paragraphs.text: Directorate-General for Justice and Consumers European Commission 1049 Bruxelles/Brussel Belgium
22 October 2025
Re: Digital Fairness Act public consultation
Contributors (in alphabetical order):
Matteo Migliaccio (LLM student, Utrecht University) Vedika Sajnani (LLM student, Utrecht University) Shanna Zipf (LLM student, Utrecht University)
To whom it may concern,

evidence_quotes.quote: social media influencers have been a focal point.

paragraphs.text: This is a response to the solicitation for public comments of the European Commission, relating to the fairness check. This response reflects the discussions of a focus group undertaken as part of the course EU Digital Innovation and Regulation in the context of the Masters Programme Law & Technology in Europe at Utrecht University, where social media influencers have been a focal point.

evidence_quotes.quote: One of the aims of the Digital Fairness acts is to tackle marketing by social media influencers.

paragraphs.text: One of the aims of the Digital Fairness acts is to tackle marketing by social media influencers. This paper explains why algorithmic detection by social media platforms should not be the main enforcement tool and why the primary legal responsibility should rest with the advertisers.

evidence_quotes.quote: misleading advertising

paragraphs.text: Algorithms should not be used to enforce prohibition on misleading advertising

evidence_quotes.quote: influencer advertising

paragraphs.text: Mandating algorithmic surveillance for infringements of consumer protection law in the context of influencer advertising carries the same risk of the YouTube Adpocalypse: platforms could choose to overmoderate content to avoid penalties incurred for not finding illegal content on their platforms. During the YouTube Adpocalypse, companies advertising on Google's YouTube platform threatened Google by pulling their advertisements when they were shown in combination with content that was deemed
¹Cyber Risk GmbH, "Digital Fairness Act (DFA) | Updates" (Digital-Fairness-Act.com, 17 September 2024) 'https://www.digital-fairness-act.com' accessed 22 October 2025

evidence_quotes.quote: remove advertisements on videos that mentioned sensitive topics.

paragraphs.text: controversial. In response, Google introduced a strict algorithmic system that would remove advertisements on videos that mentioned sensitive topics.

evidence_quotes.quote: it's likely that a user recommending products on their own initiative might be incorrectly flagged as undisclosed influencer marketing, since platforms would have to rely on algorithms to moderate content.

paragraphs.text: This example of private regulation suggests that the use of algorithms might hurt the dissemination of free speech online. The YouTube Adpocalypse has shown how content creators are pushed to self-censor for fear of being demonetized or deprioritized in recommendations. This is due to the tendency of algorithms of not being able to distinguish between the linguistic cues that shape context in the same way that humans do, and in the case of YouTube, incorrectly flagging videos as discussing sexual topics, for example. If the burden were to be exclusively placed on platforms, it's likely that a user recommending products on their own initiative might be incorrectly flagged as undisclosed influencer marketing, since platforms would have to rely on algorithms to moderate content.

evidence_quotes.quote: misleading marketing by influencers

paragraphs.text: Those who profit most from misleading marketing by influencers are the traders who's goods or services receive more outreach. They are the ones who ultimately benefit from the revenue and visibility based on unfair practices such as disguising ads as peer recommendation. Even though influencers might receive some form of compensation and platforms might get higher engagement, it is clear the trader that profits the most and should therefore bear the primary legal responsibility.

section_path: 2. Advertisers should bear the primary legal responsibility

evidence_quotes.quote: misleading marketing by influencers

paragraphs.text: Moreover, social media platforms are not in a leading position to regulate misleading marketing by influencers as they would rely on algorithms for enforcement, which as mentioned is not favorable. Similarly placing the primary legal responsibility burden on influencers is also not desirable as these would include individuals who might not have skills to enforce complex legal regulations. Additionally, expecting national consumer protection authorities to go after tens of thousands of influencers doesn't seem feasible.

section_path: 2. Advertisers should bear the primary legal responsibility

paragraphs.text: What responsibilities and expectations should the digital fairness act impose on advertisers?

section_path: 2. Advertisers should bear the primary legal responsibility

evidence_quotes.quote: influencer marketing.

paragraphs.text: The Digital Fairness Act should build on the established consumer law principles namely the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive and its 2021 Guidance which includes influencer marketing.

section_path: 2. Advertisers should bear the primary legal responsibility

evidence_quotes.quote: Traders should be held responsible and accountable when they expect, request, or reward promotional content.

paragraphs.text: Traders should be held responsible and accountable when they expect, request, or reward promotional content. This follows the rationale of the UCPD where the traders are held responsible for their marketing activity.

section_path: 2. Advertisers should bear the primary legal responsibility

evidence_quotes.quote: to include a clause obliging the content to be transparent; including the nature of the advertising and promotion.

paragraphs.text: In this context advertisers should be obliged when there is a contract for promotional activities to include a clause obliging the content to be transparent; including the nature of the advertising and promotion. This helps advertisers maintain their ‘professional diligence’ and encourages compliance with Article 5, 6 and 7 of the UCPD’s throughout the supply chain.

section_path: 2. Advertisers should bear the primary legal responsibility

evidence_quotes.quote: provide clear disclosure instructions to recipients of PR packages or of similar activities.
transparency when influencers have received any form of benefit or when their content may be biased

paragraphs.text: In cases where an advertiser sends PR packages or provides goods or services for free without any explicit expectation of promotional content or compensation, the advertiser should maintain an internal record of these distributions. Advertisers in this context may not be expected to monitor and be held responsible for the content posted, in this case the burden would fall on the ‘influencer’ or the entity who makes the post. Yet the advertiser would need to provide clear disclosure instructions to recipients of PR packages or of similar activities. This would align with the spirit of the UCPD’s which emphasizes the need for transparency when influencers have received any form of benefit or when their content may be biased, while avoiding overburdening compliance for traders and advertisers.

section_path: 2. Advertisers should bear the primary legal responsibility

paragraphs.text: Easing Compliance

section_path: 2. Advertisers should bear the primary legal responsibility

paragraphs.text: Compliance obligations should be proportionate to company size and campaign scale. Large advertisers or high-value campaigns should face stricter requirements than SMEs

section_path: 2. Advertisers should bear the primary legal responsibility

section_path: 2. Advertisers should bear the primary legal responsibility

paragraphs.text: ⁷European Commission, Guidance on the interpretation and application of Directive 2005/29/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council concerning unfair business-to-consumer commercial practices in the internal market(Notice, 17 December 2021) ‘https://commission.europa.eu/law/law-topic/consumer-protection-law/unfair-commercial-practices-and-price-indication/unfair-commercial-practices-directive_en’ accessed 22 October 2025

section_path: 2. Advertisers should bear the primary legal responsibility

paragraphs.text: ⁸Directive 2005/29/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 11 May 2005 concerning unfair business-to-consumer commercial practices in the internal market and amending Council Directive 84/450/EEC, Directives 97/7/EC, 98/27/EC and 2002/65/EC and Regulation (EC) No 2006/2004 of the European Parliament and of the Council, OJ L 149/22, 11.6.2005, art 5, 6, 7

section_path: 2. Advertisers should bear the primary legal responsibility

evidence_quotes.quote: influencer marketing remains accessible to all businesses

paragraphs.text: who conduct smaller influencer collaborations. This ensures that influencer marketing remains accessible to all businesses and does not become a luxury accessible to only major brands.

section_path: 2. Advertisers should bear the primary legal responsibility

evidence_quotes.quote: influencers stay transparent.

paragraphs.text: Moreover, to help compliance regulators should provide free templates for PR disclosure notices, contracts, and influencer guidelines. This will reduce the legal burden on SMEs, and help influencers stay transparent.

section_path: 2. Advertisers should bear the primary legal responsibility

evidence_quotes.quote: influencer behaviour and/or practices.

paragraphs.text: The primary criticism of our proposal is that there is an undue burden placed on advertisers, even in scenarios where they are unable to control or dictate influencer behaviour and/or practices.

section_path: 5. Potential Criticism and Relevant Mitigations

evidence_quotes.quote: The recent creation of influencer marketing can be considered just an extension of advertisers' commercial practices.

such that advertisers only face obligations when there exists a contractual relationship between the influencer and the advertiser, such that the advertiser has reasonable ability to influence content.
For PR packages, without explicit promotional intent, the obligations for advertisers are limited to record-keeping.

paragraphs.text: However, advertisers already bear the responsibility for their commercial advertising practices under the UCPD. The recent creation of influencer marketing can be considered just an extension of advertisers' commercial practices. Moreover, to mitigate the risk of burdening advertisers with practices beyond their control, the measures must be proportional, such that advertisers only face obligations when there exists a contractual relationship between the influencer and the advertiser, such that the advertiser has reasonable ability to influence content. For PR packages, without explicit promotional intent, the obligations for advertisers are limited to record-keeping.

section_path: 5. Potential Criticism and Relevant Mitigations

evidence_quotes.quote: influencers are not being regulated.
disclosure obligations for advertisements.

paragraphs.text: Secondly, another criticism of the proposed framework is that influencers are not being regulated. However, influencers are bound to existing prohibitions on misleading advertising, and have disclosure obligations for advertisements. Moreover, the motive for such distribution of obligations is for the objective of pragmatic enforcement. Consumer protection authorities cannot feasibly pursue thousands of individual influencers, however, they can regulate a, comparatively, much smaller number of advertisers.

section_path: 5. Potential Criticism and Relevant Mitigations

evidence_quotes.quote: influencers, who are paid a fixed sum of money for their advertising activity.

paragraphs.text: This approach accounts for the resources available to, and profits earned by advertisers, in comparison to influencers, who are paid a fixed sum of money for their advertising activity. Objectively, it is easier for advertisers to comply with such a framework, due to their access to resources, which can be allocated to regulatory compliance. Expecting advertisers to bear this burden, over influencers, is proportionate considering that such misleading advertising practices benefit advertisers more in the long-term.

section_path: 5. Potential Criticism and Relevant Mitigations

paragraphs.text: Firstly, it is necessary to ensure that the proposed framework is proportional and necessary to achieve the objectives pursued by the DFA. Regulatory burdens should primarily fall upon those who are best positioned to ensure regulatory compliance. In light

section_path: 6. Guiding Principles of the Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: in influencer marketing.

paragraphs.text: of this, this proposal acknowledges that advertisers and macro influencers (with a wider audience and impact) possess the resources, expertise, and/or commercial incentive to ensure fair practices in influencer marketing.

section_path: 6. Guiding Principles of the Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: micro and macro influencers

paragraphs.text: Secondly, it is important to acknowledge that regulatory instruments are only effective, if they can be enforced. A framework which requires authorities to pursue thousands of micro and macro influencers, or relies on imperfect algorithmic detection, cannot achieve its objectives in practice. In recognition of this, the proposed framework creates a viable enforcement pathway, by concentrating primary liability for marketing practices on a more limited number of advertisers.

section_path: 6. Guiding Principles of the Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: influencer marketing is a form of marketing by traders

paragraphs.text: Moreover, it is important to maintain cohesion with existing regulatory frameworks. The UCPD establishes that traders bear the responsibility for their marketing practices. Since influencer marketing is a form of marketing by traders, the DFA should contribute to extension of the existing UCPD framework, rather than creating a parallel regime.

section_path: 6. Guiding Principles of the Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: unfair undisclosed advertisements by influencers.
transparency and fairness.

paragraphs.text: Lastly, the aim pursued by this proposal is to create fair market conditions. Consumers are entitled to make informed choices, and not be deceived by unfair undisclosed advertisements by influencers. Therefore, by placing the responsibility on advertisers, as the actors that benefit from and control influencer marketing campaigns, the DFA can ensure that influencer marketing operates in alignment with principles of transparency and fairness.

section_path: 6. Guiding Principles of the Digital Fairness Act
